narrative:
Short impact summary

The COVID-19 CT Lung and Infection Segmentation Dataset, published in 2020, has established itself as a crucial resource in the study of COVID-19, demonstrated by its 238 citations and nearly 48,300 downloads. It serves as a foundational dataset, fostering significant scholarly uptake, particularly in fields such as artificial intelligence and computer science.

Full narrative

Released by Jun Ma and collaborators, the dataset has become a reference backbone in COVID-19 research, heralded for its role in advancing medical imaging analysis. Its impact is underscored by high visibility, evidenced by its citations across various reputable platforms, including arXiv and bioRxiv. The dataset has been cited in diverse works, reinforcing its relevance across multiple disciplines, particularly in artificial intelligence applications for medical diagnostics.

The dataset has not only fostered academic inquiry but has also proven to be an infrastructure dataset. It has been integrated into studies appearing in 15 distinct scholarly venues, reflecting its adoption across different contexts and reinforcing its utility for researchers and practitioners alike. The ongoing academic discourse has positioned the dataset as a significant contributor to the understanding and management of COVID-19 related lung infections.

In terms of its societal impact, the dataset is linked to the Sustainable Development Goal pertaining to Zero Hunger, indicating its broader implications beyond immediate research. Furthermore, the 243 instances of reuse, spanning articles, preprints, and various types of academic content, exhibit an active engagement with the dataset, underscoring its role in advancing scientific knowledge during the pandemic.

Overall, the COVID-19 CT Lung and Infection Segmentation Dataset exemplifies a critical resource that not only supports research in virology and medicine but also intersects with technological innovations in artificial intelligence, contributing to the ongoing response to the global health crisis.
